quiz_questions.question_text: Concentration measures:

options.text: The temperature of a solution at a given time
The color or appearance of a solution mixture
The volume of solvent used in the mixture only
The amount of solute in a given amount of solution

quiz_questions.explanation: Concentration measures how much solute is present in a given quantity of solution or solvent. It can be expressed in units like g/L, percent, molarity, or ppm.

quiz_questions.question_text: A concentrated solution has:

options.text: Very little solute dissolved in the solvent present
No solute at all dissolved in the solvent present
A large amount of solute relative to solvent amount
Only solvent with no solute dissolved in the mixture

quiz_questions.explanation: A concentrated solution contains a large amount of solute relative to the amount of solvent. The solution has a high concentration.

quiz_questions.question_text: A dilute solution has:

options.text: No solvent present in the solution mixture at all
Solid particles that are visible floating in solution
A small amount of solute relative to solvent amount
A very large amount of solute dissolved in the solvent

quiz_questions.explanation: A dilute solution contains a small amount of solute relative to the amount of solvent. The solution has a low concentration.

quiz_questions.question_text: Parts per million (ppm) is used for:

options.text: Very small concentrations of solute in solution
Very large concentrations of solute in solution
Only gas-phase concentrations and nothing else
Only solid-phase concentrations and nothing else

quiz_questions.explanation: Parts per million (ppm) is a concentration unit used for very small amounts of solute, such as measuring pollutants in water or contaminants in food.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which solution is more concentrated: 5 g/L or 10 g/L?

options.text: They are equal in concentration level
Cannot determine from this information alone
5 g/L is more concentrated than 10 g/L
10 g/L is more concentrated than 5 g/L

quiz_questions.explanation: 10 g/L has more solute per liter of solution than 5 g/L, making it the more concentrated solution.

quiz_questions.question_text: Mass percent is calculated as:

options.text: (Mass of solute / Mass of solution) x 100
Mass of solution divided by 100 only
(Mass of solvent / Mass of solute) x 100
Mass of solute multiplied by 100 only

quiz_questions.explanation: Mass percent = (mass of solute / mass of solution) x 100%. The mass of solution equals mass of solute plus mass of solvent.

quiz_questions.question_text: If 10 g of sugar is dissolved in 90 g of water, the mass percent of sugar is:

options.text: About 9% sugar by mass in the solution
About 90% sugar by mass in the solution
About 11% sugar by mass in the solution
About 10% sugar by mass in the solution

quiz_questions.explanation: Mass percent = (10 g / (10 g + 90 g)) x 100 = (10/100) x 100 = 10%. The total solution mass is 100 g.

quiz_questions.question_text: Volume percent is used when:

options.text: Both solute and solvent are solids in the mixture
Both solute and solvent are liquids in the mixture
The solute is a gas and the solvent is a solid material
The concentration is extremely low at parts per million

quiz_questions.explanation: Volume percent is typically used when both the solute and solvent are liquids, such as in alcoholic beverages where the alcohol content is given as volume percent.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 5% salt solution means:

options.text: 5 g of salt is dissolved in 100 g of water only
5 g of salt per 100 g of total solution mixture
5 mL of salt per 1000 mL of water solvent only
5 moles of salt per liter of total solution volume

quiz_questions.explanation: A 5% salt solution means 5 grams of salt per 100 grams of total solution. Percent concentration is always relative to the total solution, not just the solvent.

quiz_questions.question_text: Increasing the amount of solute in a fixed volume of solvent will:

options.text: Decrease the concentration of the resulting solution
Have no effect on the concentration of the solution
Increase the concentration of the resulting solution
Change the solvent into a different chemical substance

quiz_questions.explanation: Adding more solute to the same volume of solvent increases the ratio of solute to solution, which increases the concentration.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the definition of concentration in chemistry?

options.text: The total mass of solvent in a solution mixture
The measure of how much solute is in a given amount of solution
The rate at which a solute dissolves in a particular solvent
The temperature at which a solution becomes saturated fully

quiz_questions.explanation: Concentration is a measure of the amount of solute present in a given quantity of solution. Higher concentration means more solute per unit of solution.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'dilute' mean when describing a solution?

options.text: The solution contains a large amount of solute per unit volume
The solution contains a small amount of solute per unit volume
The solution has been heated to its boiling point temperature
The solution has been cooled to its freezing point temperature

quiz_questions.explanation: A dilute solution has a relatively small amount of solute compared to the amount of solvent, resulting in a low concentration.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'concentrated' mean when describing a solution?

options.text: The solution has no solute dissolved in the solvent at all
The solution has a large amount of solute per unit volume
The solution cannot dissolve any more solvent molecules
The solution has been filtered to remove all the solute

quiz_questions.explanation: A concentrated solution has a relatively large amount of solute compared to the amount of solvent, resulting in a high concentration.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does parts per billion (ppb) measure?

options.text: Very large concentrations of common laboratory chemicals
Extremely small concentrations like trace contaminants in water
The number of molecules in exactly one mole of a substance
The volume of gas produced in a chemical reaction at STP

quiz_questions.explanation: Parts per billion (ppb) is used to measure extremely small concentrations, even smaller than ppm. It is commonly used for trace pollutants and contaminants.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'mass/volume percent' express?

options.text: Grams of solute per 100 mL of solution expressed as a percent
Liters of solvent per kilogram of total solution as a percent
Moles of solute per liter of solvent expressed as a percentage
Grams of solvent per 100 mL of solute expressed as a percent

quiz_questions.explanation: Mass/volume percent expresses the mass of solute (in grams) per 100 mL of solution as a percentage. It is commonly used in pharmaceutical and medical contexts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Define the term 'saturated solution' in terms of concentration.

options.text: A solution that can still dissolve more solute at that temperature
A solution that holds the maximum amount of dissolved solute possible
A solution that has had all its solvent evaporated away completely
A solution that contains no solute dissolved at the given temperature

quiz_questions.explanation: A saturated solution holds the maximum amount of solute that can dissolve at a given temperature. Adding more solute will not increase concentration further.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'g/L' stand for as a concentration unit?

options.text: Grams of solvent per liter of pure solute only
Grams of solute per liter of total solution volume
Gallons of liquid per liter of container space used
Grams of solution per liter of the pure solvent only

quiz_questions.explanation: The unit g/L means grams of solute per liter of solution. It is a common way to express concentration, especially in everyday and medical contexts.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'percent concentration' mean?

options.text: The fraction of solute in a solution expressed as parts per hundred
The temperature at which exactly one percent of solute dissolves
The number of moles of solute that equals one percent of the total
The pressure needed to dissolve exactly one percent of the solute

quiz_questions.explanation: Percent concentration expresses the proportion of solute in a solution as parts per hundred. It can be mass/mass, mass/volume, or volume/volume percent.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'stock solution' mean in laboratory chemistry?

options.text: A very dilute solution ready for immediate use in an experiment
A concentrated solution that can be diluted to desired concentrations
A solution that has been stored past its expiration date in the lab
A solution that contains only water with no solute dissolved in it

quiz_questions.explanation: A stock solution is a concentrated solution kept in a laboratory that can be diluted with solvent to make solutions of lower concentrations as needed for experiments.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the difference between qualitative and quantitative descriptions of concentration?

options.text: There is no real difference between these two descriptions
Qualitative uses words like dilute/concentrated; quantitative uses exact numbers
Qualitative uses exact numbers while quantitative uses general words
Both always require numerical values and precise measurements

quiz_questions.explanation: Qualitative descriptions use words like 'dilute' or 'concentrated' without exact numbers. Quantitative descriptions use precise numerical values like 0.5 M or 10%.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student makes two solutions: Solution A has 5 g of salt in 100 mL, and Solution B has 15 g of salt in 100 mL. Which is more concentrated?

options.text: Solution A because it was made first in the experiment
They are equally concentrated since both use 100 mL
Solution B because it has more solute in the same volume
Solution A because less solute means higher concentration

quiz_questions.explanation: Solution B is more concentrated because it has three times more solute (15 g vs 5 g) dissolved in the same volume of solution (100 mL).

quiz_questions.question_text: A student dissolves 20 g of NaCl in 80 g of water. What is the mass percent of NaCl in this solution?

options.text: 25% because 20 divided by 80 times 100 equals 25
20% because 20 divided by 100 times 100 equals 20
80% because water is most of the solution by mass
10% because the mass of the solute must be halved

quiz_questions.explanation: Mass percent = (mass of solute / mass of solution) x 100 = (20 g / (20 g + 80 g)) x 100 = (20/100) x 100 = 20%.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student adds water to a dark-colored juice concentrate. The color becomes lighter. What happened to the concentration?

options.text: The concentration increased because more liquid was added
The concentration decreased because more solvent diluted the solute
The concentration stayed the same because no solute was removed
The concentration cannot change by simply adding water to it

quiz_questions.explanation: Adding water (solvent) to the concentrate increases the total volume while the amount of solute stays the same, decreasing the concentration and making the color lighter.

quiz_questions.question_text: A lab technician needs to measure the fluoride concentration in drinking water at 0.7 ppm. Why is ppm an appropriate unit here?

options.text: Because the concentration is too large for regular percent units
Because ppm measures very small concentrations like trace amounts
Because ppm is the only unit that works for measuring liquids
Because ppm is easier to calculate than any other unit of measure

quiz_questions.explanation: Fluoride in drinking water is present in very small amounts. PPM is appropriate for trace concentrations; 0.7 ppm = 0.00007%, which would be awkward to express as a percent.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student evaporates 200 mL of a salt solution and collects 10 g of salt. What was the concentration in g/L?

options.text: 20 g/L of salt was dissolved in the solution
50 g/L of salt was dissolved in the solution
10 g/L of salt was dissolved in the solution
100 g/L of salt was dissolved in the solution

quiz_questions.explanation: 200 mL = 0.2 L. Concentration = 10 g / 0.2 L = 50 g/L. The student had 10 grams of salt in 0.2 liters of solution.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student makes sweet tea by adding 3 tablespoons of sugar. Her friend adds 6 tablespoons of sugar to the same amount of tea. Whose tea is more concentrated?

options.text: The first student's tea because less sugar means more concentrated
Both are equally concentrated since they used the same amount of tea
The friend's tea because it has more solute in the same amount of solvent
Neither is concentrated because tea is not a true chemical solution

quiz_questions.explanation: The friend's tea is more concentrated because more sugar (solute) was dissolved in the same volume of tea (solvent), giving a higher solute-to-solution ratio.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student reads that a saline IV solution is 0.9% NaCl. What does this mean in practical terms?

options.text: There are 0.9 grams of NaCl in every 100 grams of the solution
There are 0.9 liters of NaCl in every 100 liters of the solution
There are 9 grams of NaCl dissolved in every gram of the water
There are 0.9 moles of NaCl dissolved in every liter of the solution

quiz_questions.explanation: A 0.9% NaCl solution means 0.9 grams of NaCl per 100 grams (or approximately 100 mL) of solution. This is the standard concentration for normal saline used in IVs.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student boils a sugar solution on a hot plate until half the water evaporates. What happens to the sugar concentration?

options.text: The concentration stays the same because the sugar amount is unchanged
The concentration approximately doubles because half the solvent was removed
The concentration decreases because heat destroys the sugar molecules
The concentration drops to zero because all the sugar evaporated away

quiz_questions.explanation: When half the water evaporates, the amount of sugar stays the same but the volume of solution is halved. Since concentration = solute/solution, the concentration approximately doubles.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student has two bottles: one labeled '1 M HCl' and another labeled '6 M HCl.' The student needs a dilute acid for the experiment. Which should be chosen?

options.text: 6 M HCl because a higher number means it is more dilute
1 M HCl because a lower molarity means it is more dilute
Either one because both concentrations are exactly the same
Neither one because HCl cannot be used in a dilute form

quiz_questions.explanation: 1 M HCl is more dilute than 6 M HCl because it has fewer moles of solute per liter. Lower molarity means lower concentration.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student prepares a solution of 25 g of KNO3 in 75 g of water. She needs to report the mass percent. What value should she report?

options.text: 33% because 25 divided by 75 equals about 0.33
25% because 25 divided by 100 equals 0.25 times 100
75% because the solvent makes up most of the solution
50% because the solute and solvent are roughly equal amounts

quiz_questions.explanation: Mass percent = (25 g / (25 g + 75 g)) x 100 = (25/100) x 100 = 25%. Total solution mass is 100 g.
